original_abstract: AI code generators, such as OpenAI Codex, offer promising support for novice programmers by translating natural language into functional code. However, concerns remain that an over-reliance on these tools could hinder long-term learning and retention. To investigate these implications within introductory programming, we conducted a controlled experiment involving 69 students (ages 10–17). Participants completed 45 Python code-authoring tasks—with half the group using Codex—followed by subsequent code-modification challenges. Our findings indicate that Codex significantly boosted authoring performance, resulting in a 1.15x higher completion rate and scores 1.8x higher than the control group, without undermining performance on manual modification tasks. Furthermore, learners who used Codex during training performed slightly better on evaluation post-tests held one week later, though this trend was not statistically significant. Notably, students with higher initial Scratch proficiency showed significantly better retention if they had prior access to Codex, suggesting that AI assistance may particularly benefit those with existing foundational logic.
humanized_abstract: The impact of an AI code generator on learning and retention in introductory programming Background: Researchers have developed several AI code generators to aid novice programmers, such as OpenAI Codex. The ultimate goal of such systems is to help programmers write a large amount of correct code. One concern of using such tools is that they can impede learning and retention of programming skills, which is crucial to the success of introductory programming courses. To investigate this issue, we conducted a controlled study with 69 students aged between 10 and 17 years old from an after-school centre located in Sydney. Background information of the students is shown in Table I. Our study had 69 students working on 45 Python code-authoring tasks. The students were randomly assigned into two groups with the Codex group writing code with the aid of OpenAI Codex and the control group writing code on their own. Students in both groups worked on the same 45 tasks and, after completing all the tasks, worked on 10 code-modification tasks. The results show that students using Codex have a 1.15x increase in authoring tasks and a 1.8x increase in score. The increased use of Codex did not negatively impact students’ ability to complete the modification tasks. Although students who used Codex during training only marginally outperformed those in the control condition in the evaluation post-test held one week later, this is not statistically significant. Students with higher Scratch skills benefited from more retention when they had access to Codex before the post-test.

To investigate this issue, we conducted a controlled study with 69 students aged between 10 and 17 years old from an after-school centre located in Sydney. Background information of the students is shown in Table I. Our study had 69 students working on 45 Python code-authoring tasks. The students were randomly assigned into two groups with the Codex group writing code with the aid of OpenAI Codex and the control group writing code on their own. Students in both groups worked on the same 45 tasks and, after completing all the tasks, worked on 10 code-modification tasks. The increased use of Codex did not negatively impact students’ ability to complete the modification tasks. Although students who used Codex during training only marginally outperformed those in the control condition in the evaluation post-test held one week later, this is not statistically significant. Students with higher Scratch skills benefited from more retention when they had access to Codex before the post-test.
document.input: AI code generators, such as OpenAI Codex, offer promising support for novice programmers by translating natural language into functional code. However, concerns remain that an over-reliance on these tools could hinder long-term learning and retention. To investigate these implications within introductory programming, we conducted a controlled experiment involving 69 students (ages 10–17). Participants completed 45 Python code-authoring tasks—with half the group using Codex—followed by subsequent code-modification challenges. Our findings indicate that Codex significantly boosted authoring performance, resulting in a 1.15x higher completion rate and scores 1.8x higher than the control group, without undermining performance on manual modification tasks. Furthermore, learners who used Codex during training performed slightly better on evaluation post-tests held one week later, though this trend was not statistically significant. Notably, students with higher initial Scratch proficiency showed significantly better retention if they had prior access to Codex, suggesting that AI assistance may particularly benefit those with existing foundational logic.
